description: Quality rules for arcade-style action games — sprite anatomy (ag-016), attack animations (ag-017), keyboard input, rAF loop, collision, off-screen spawn (ag-018). Covers named-game canaries: Frogger, Pac-Man, Joust, Defender, Galaxian, Asteroids, Centipede, Q*bert, Kaboom, BurgerTime, Lode Runner, Spy Hunter, Pole Position, Skee-Ball, Pinball, Tempest, Adventure, Boxing, Snake, Breakout, Pong, Tetris.

guidelines.rule: The game must respond to keyboard input immediately. Use keydown events (not keypress) for game controls. Arrow keys, WASD, or Space should work.
guidelines.why: Arcade games need instant responsive controls. Laggy or non-functional controls make the game unplayable.
examples: document.addEventListener('keydown', handleKey)
preventDefault() on arrow keys to stop page scrolling

guidelines.rule: Use requestAnimationFrame for the game loop, NOT setInterval. Target 60fps. Delta-time based movement for consistent speed.
guidelines.why: setInterval causes inconsistent frame rates and janky movement. rAF syncs with the display refresh.
examples: function gameLoop(timestamp) { update(dt); render(); requestAnimationFrame(gameLoop); }

guidelines.rule: Show score, lives, and level prominently. Include a game-over screen with final score and restart option.
guidelines.why: Players need constant feedback on their performance.
examples: HUD at top: Score: 1250 | Lives: 3 | Level: 2
Game Over overlay with 'Press Space to restart'

guidelines.rule: Implement proper collision detection. For rectangles use AABB, for circles use distance checks.
guidelines.why: Bad collision detection — objects passing through each other or false collisions — ruins gameplay.
examples: AABB: if (a.x < b.x+b.w && a.x+a.w > b.x && a.y < b.y+b.h && a.y+a.h > b.y)

guidelines.rule: Snake game MUST grow correctly when eating food, and MUST end when the snake hits a wall or its own body. The snake body segments MUST follow the head exactly — each segment moves to where the previous segment was. Food MUST appear at a random empty cell, never on the snake body.
guidelines.why: Common Snake bugs: snake passes through walls instead of dying, body segments don't follow correctly creating gaps, food spawns on snake body, score doesn't increase on eat.
examples: Grow: on eat, push new tail segment instead of removing old tail
Self-collision: head position matches any body segment = game over
Food spawn: filter out cells occupied by snake body

guidelines.rule: Minesweeper MUST implement: left-click to reveal a cell, right-click to toggle flag, flood-fill auto-reveal for empty cells (cells with 0 adjacent mines), correct mine count in adjacent cells (1-8), first-click safety (never a mine on the first click), win condition (all non-mine cells revealed).
guidelines.why: Minesweeper without flood-fill is essentially unplayable. First-click safety is essential — dying on move 1 is not fun.
examples: Flood-fill: when revealing a 0-cell, recursively reveal all 8 neighbours
First-click: place mines AFTER first click, excluding clicked cell and its neighbours
Flag: right-click toggles 🚩, flagged cells cannot be revealed by left-click

guidelines.rule: Connect Four MUST alternate turns between Red and Yellow, check for 4-in-a-row in all 4 directions (horizontal, vertical, diagonal ↗, diagonal ↘) after every move, highlight the winning 4 chips, and prevent moves into full columns.
guidelines.why: Games that don't check diagonals allow undetected wins. Overflowing a full column corrupts board state.
examples: Check all 4 directions from last-placed chip
Highlight winning chips: add CSS class .win to the 4 winning cells
Column full: disable click on that column or show visual indicator

guidelines.rule: Battleship MUST have a 10×10 grid for each player. Ship placement (5 ships: Carrier×5, Battleship×4, Cruiser×3, Submarine×3, Destroyer×2) must validate no overlaps and no out-of-bounds. AI opponent fires intelligently after a hit (target adjacent cells). Show hit/miss on the opponent's board with contrasting colors.
guidelines.why: Battleship without hit-targeting AI is trivially easy. Ship placement validation prevents impossible board states.
examples: After AI hits, push adjacent cells into a target queue before reverting to random
Hit = red, Miss = white dot on dark, Sunk ship reveals all cells of that ship
Show both grids: 'Your Fleet' (ships visible) and 'Enemy Waters' (ships hidden)

guidelines.rule: Simon Says / Memory sequence game MUST show the sequence with clear color flashes (each color lights up with a 400ms on / 200ms off pattern), play a distinct tone per color, accept user input ONLY after the sequence finishes playing, detect wrong input immediately and show game-over, increase sequence by 1 each successful round.
guidelines.why: Simon clones frequently play the sequence and accept input simultaneously, making it impossible to follow. Or they don't detect wrong input until the sequence ends.
examples: Use AudioContext to play: 261Hz (green), 329Hz (red), 392Hz (yellow), 523Hz (blue)
Disable buttons while showing sequence: buttons.forEach(b => b.disabled = true)
Show current round: 'Round 5 — sequence of 5 colors'

guidelines.rule: Arcade games MUST save high scores to localStorage AND submit to the global leaderboard on game-over using `window.mChatAI?.leaderboard?.submit({ score: finalScore, metadata: { level, lives_lost } })`. Show the user's global rank on the game-over screen. Render a top-10 leaderboard table with names and scores.
guidelines.why: Local high scores vanish on reinstall and provide no competitive incentive. Leaderboard turns 'I beat my score' into 'I beat everyone'. Zero-config with the mChatAI bridge — 3 lines of code.
examples: Game over: leaderboard.submit({ score, metadata: { level: currentLevel, waveReached: wave } }).then(r => showGameOver(r.rank, r.percentile));
Top-10 table: leaderboard.getTop(10).then(scores => { scores.forEach((s,i) => addRow(i+1, s.displayName, s.score)); });
localStorage fallback: always save to localStorage too, show local best even without account

guidelines.rule: Atari/retro-style games MUST use a pixel-art aesthetic: chunky pixels rendered on a canvas using integer coordinates, a limited palette of 4-16 bold colors (no gradients or anti-aliasing), blocky sans-serif font (monospace or bitmap-style), and scanline or CRT overlay (optional but strongly recommended). The canvas should fill the viewport with `imageSmoothingEnabled = false`.
guidelines.why: Atari-style games that use modern smooth CSS gradients, rounded corners, or system fonts break the retro illusion completely. The pixelated aesthetic IS the product — without it the game feels like a bad clone rather than a loving tribute.
examples: ctx.imageSmoothingEnabled = false; — must be set on every canvas resize
Canvas scale: draw at 320×240 internal resolution, CSS-scale up to fill the screen
Palette: use Atari 2600-style colors — black #000, white #FFF, orange #F77F00, cyan #00FFFF, green #00FF00, red #FF0000, purple #7700FF
Font: use 'Courier New' or embed a pixel font via @font-face
Scanlines: draw semi-transparent horizontal lines every 2px over the canvas for CRT effect
Sound: use AudioContext square-wave oscillators (type:'square') for authentic beeps, not sine waves

guidelines.rule: Atari-style Space Invaders / Galaxian MUST: render the player ship as a pixel-art sprite at the bottom, alien grid of 5×11 marching left/right and descending, aliens shoot back at increasing rate as their count decreases, bunkers erode pixel-by-pixel when hit, UFO bonus target crosses top. Player has 3 lives shown as mini ship sprites.
guidelines.why: Space Invaders clones without alien return fire, descending movement, or bunker erosion miss the core tension. The game gets harder as you kill aliens — fewer aliens move faster — this is the key difficulty curve.
examples: Alien march speed: baseSpeed * (totalAliens / startingAliens) — gets faster as aliens die
Alien descent: move down by alienHeight when hitting a wall
Bunkers: use a pixel buffer (ImageData) that removes individual pixels on bullet hit
3 alien types: top row = 30pts, middle rows = 20pts, bottom rows = 10pts
UFO: appears at top every 25-30 seconds, worth 50-300 random points

guidelines.rule: Atari-style Asteroids MUST: render vector-style ship (triangle outline), implement screen wrapping (objects exit one side and enter the other), ship thrust with momentum/inertia (no instant stop), asteroids split into 2 smaller ones when shot (large→medium→small→destroyed), ship is invincible for 3 seconds after respawn, hyperspace (random teleport) as emergency escape.
guidelines.why: Asteroids without screen-wrapping and inertia feels completely wrong — these are the two defining physics properties of the game. Asteroids that disappear instead of splitting remove the core difficulty escalation.
examples: Wrapping: if (x < 0) x = canvas.width; if (x > canvas.width) x = 0; (same for y)
Inertia: vx += Math.cos(angle) * thrust * dt; x += vx * dt; vx *= 0.99; (friction)
Split: large asteroid (r=40) → 2 medium (r=20); medium → 2 small (r=10); small → destroyed
Respawn: new ship at center, 3s invincibility shown by blinking

guidelines.rule: Atari Adventure-style games MUST: implement a multi-room dungeon map (at least 20 rooms connected by doors), a player avatar (blocky square or arrow), items the player can pick up and carry (sword, key, bridge), enemies that chase the player with simple pathfinding, and a win condition (reach the chalice). Rooms must persist state (items stay where dropped). Include a map legend or mini-map.
guidelines.why: Adventure (Atari 2600) is the ancestor of all action-adventure games. Its defining features — inventory, multi-room exploration, key-locked doors — must all be present. Without them it's just a maze game.
examples: Room connections: roomData[id].exits = { north: 5, south: 2, east: null, west: 8 }
Inventory: player can carry 1 item; picking up a new item drops the current one
Dragons: patrol a room, reverse direction at walls, chase player when visible
Sword: kills dragons on contact when player is holding it
Keys: unlock castle doors (colored door requires matching colored key)
Bridge: allows crossing walls (place it against a wall to pass through)

guidelines.rule: Wave-based / level-based shooters MUST implement a level-clear → next-wave transition. When all enemies on the current wave are destroyed, IMMEDIATELY advance to the next wave: respawn enemies (with increased difficulty: more enemies, faster spawn, or new behavior), bump a wave counter visible in the HUD, and reset the player's position if appropriate. The game cannot be 'won' on a single wave alone — there must be a sense of progression.
guidelines.why: Galaga + Centipede first-pass canaries 2026-04-25 both shipped with terminal-level-1 bugs: clearing all enemies on level 1 left an empty playfield with no progression. Players had nothing to do. The level-clear → next-wave loop is a defining property of arcade shooters; without it, they're not games, they're tech demos. Easy to overlook because LLMs render the FIRST wave and assume the loop is implicit.
examples: Wave transition: function checkWaveClear() { if (enemies.length === 0 && bullets.length === 0) startNextWave(); }
Wave scaling: function startNextWave() { wave++; const enemyCount = 10 + wave * 2; const enemySpeed = baseSpeed * (1 + wave * 0.15); spawnEnemies(enemyCount, enemySpeed); }

HUD wave counter:
Wave {wave}
updated every frame
Brief pause between waves: setTimeout(startNextWave, 1500) so player can breathe
Boss wave optional: every 5 waves, spawn 1 large boss with HP > 1
guidelines.originated: 2026-04-25 first-pass beta of galaga-079 + centipede-080 — both stuck on level 1

guidelines.rule: When the goal NAMES a recognizable arcade game by title (Atari Boxing, Frogger, Pac-Man, Joust, Defender, Pole Position, Q*bert, Kaboom, BurgerTime, Lode Runner, Spy Hunter, Skee-Ball, Pinball, Galaxian, Centipede, Tempest, etc.), the player avatar and primary game-objects MUST be MULTI-PART COMPOSITE SPRITES that visually match that game's iconic silhouette — NOT solid-color rectangles or circles. Minimum bar: ≥4 visually distinct parts (head + body + 2 limbs for figures; cab + wheels + windshield for vehicles; ladders + platforms + chef + components for BurgerTime). A 'red square vs green square' fails this rule for every named game in this list.
guidelines.why: Real incident 2026-04-27: Atari Boxing canary 140 first-pass shipped two solid-fill rectangles (red, green) standing in for the boxers. The original Atari 2600 Boxing rendered each boxer as a stick figure with HEAD + BODY + LEFT-ARM + RIGHT-ARM, where each ARM extends and retracts to throw a punch (the punch animation IS the gameplay readout — you can see who's exposed). Without recognizable anatomy, the gameplay loop is unreadable; without the extending-arm visual, 'punch' has no visual signal. Same pattern threatens any named-game canary. This rule forces the planner/generator to plan the sprite anatomy as a deliberate spec line, not an afterthought. (Second-turn auto-iteration on the same canary produced proper stick-figure boxers — proving the wizard CAN do it when the rule is pulled into context; this rule makes that the default, not the recovery.)
examples: // CORRECT: Atari Boxing sprite anatomy
// player parts: { head: circle r=8 at (cx, cy-22), body: rect 6x16 at (cx-3, cy-14),
//                 leftArm: rect 4x10 at (cx-7, cy-10) extends to (cx-15, cy-10) on left-jab,
//                 rightArm: rect 4x10 at (cx+3, cy-10) extends to (cx+11, cy-10) on right-jab }
// punch animation = arm rect extends 8px outward over 100ms then retracts
// CORRECT: Frogger frog anatomy
// { body: rounded-rect green 16x12, head: circle 8x8 forward, eyes: 2 black 2x2 dots,
//   legs: 4 small triangles at corners, animated to alternate fold/extend on hop }
// CORRECT: Pac-Man
// { body: yellow circle r=12 with mouth = pie-slice cut from -45° to +45°, animated open/close at 8Hz }
// BAD
// const player = { x, y, w: 30, h: 30, color: 'green' };  ctx.fillRect(...)
// (a green rectangle is not Frogger. A red rectangle is not a boxer.)
guidelines.originated: 2026-04-27 boxing-140 canary — turn-1 was two solid-fill squares with no anatomy; turn-2 self-corrected to stick figures (proving capability), but the default should be anatomy-from-the-start

guidelines.rule: Boxing / fighting / 1v1-melee games MUST show ATTACK ANIMATIONS that visibly extend the attacking limb away from the body (left jab = left arm extends 8-12px outward over 80-120ms then retracts; power punch = arm extends further with telegraph wind-up frames). The opponent's hit-reaction MUST be visible (head flinches back 4-6px, body flashes white for 100ms, OR a 'POW!' text burst appears). Without visible attack + reaction, players can't tell if a punch landed and fights become guess-and-check.
guidelines.why: Boxing-140 canary 2026-04-27 first-pass had 'punch' as a state-toggle with score ticker but no visible attack animation — the player saw their score tick up but couldn't see the punch happen. Same gap threatens every fighting/melee entry. Genre convention from Atari Boxing → Punch-Out → every modern fighter: arm extends OUT to throw, opponent flinches BACK on hit, score updates AFTER the visible exchange. The visual sequence IS the gameplay — without it, players can't read the fight.
examples: // CORRECT: jab animation (60fps, ~6 frames = 100ms)
function startJab(boxer, dir) {  // dir = 1 (right) or -1 (left)
boxer.armExtension = 0;
const tween = setInterval(() => {
boxer.armExtension += 2 * dir;  // extend 2px per frame
if (Math.abs(boxer.armExtension) >= 10) {
clearInterval(tween);
// hit-detection at peak extension
if (overlap(boxer.fistBox, opponent.bodyBox)) registerHit(opponent);
retract(boxer);  // separate tween bringing arm back
// CORRECT: hit reaction
function registerHit(target) {
target.flashWhite = true; setTimeout(() => target.flashWhite = false, 100);
target.headOffset.x -= 5;  // flinch back
setTimeout(() => target.headOffset.x = 0, 150);
target.score = Math.max(0, target.score - 1);
spawnPow(target.x, target.y - 20);  // 'POW!' text burst
// BAD: invisible punch — score ticks but nothing visible happens
// if (button === 'punch') { opponent.score--; }
guidelines.originated: 2026-04-27 boxing-140 — punch was state-toggle with score change, no visible attack on first pass

guidelines.rule: Side-scrolling and lane-traffic spawners (cars in Frogger, ghosts in Pac-Man, fish in Fishing Derby, vehicles in Spy Hunter, asteroids in Defender, falling bombs in Kaboom, missiles in Missile Command) MUST spawn entities OFF-SCREEN, then scroll them ON-SCREEN at their lane velocity. Spawning AT random visible positions (e.g. `entity.x = Math.random() * canvas.width`) causes cars/logs/enemies to BLINK INTO EXISTENCE in front of the player — unfair, unreadable, and breaks genre convention. Standard pattern: spawn at `x = (lane.direction > 0) ? -entity.width : canvas.width`; despawn at the opposite edge with `+ entity.width` margin so they fully exit before recycling.
guidelines.why: Real incident 2026-04-27: Frogger-pond canary first-pass had cars + logs popping into existence at random visible X positions instead of scrolling on from off-screen. Player reported 'I just see a car pop up' — fair-play impossible because entities materialize without warning. Same anti-pattern threatens every lane-traffic / scrolling-spawner game. Genre convention from the original Atari Frogger (1981), Pac-Man (1980), and every modern endless-runner: visible entities ALWAYS originate from beyond the screen edge in their lane's direction. The bug is easy to write because `Math.random() * canvas.width` is the obvious-looking 'random spawn' code; the test is whether the LLM remembers the off-screen anchor.
examples: // CORRECT: lane-aware off-screen spawn
function spawnCar(lane) {
const dir = lane.direction;  // +1 = moves right, -1 = moves left
const x = dir > 0 ? -CAR_W : canvas.width;  // start fully off-screen on the entry edge
cars.push({ x, y: lane.y, vx: dir * lane.speed, w: CAR_W, h: CAR_H, lane });
// CORRECT: despawn after car has fully exited the opposite edge
function tickCar(c, dt) {
c.x += c.vx * dt;
if (c.vx > 0 && c.x > canvas.width + 4) return false;  // off right edge → recycle
if (c.vx < 0 && c.x < -c.w - 4) return false;          // off left edge → recycle
return true;
// BAD: random visible spawn — entities pop into existence in front of the player
// const x = Math.random() * canvas.width;  // ← causes blink-in
// CORRECT: spawn cadence (Poisson-like) decoupled from spawn position
if (lane.spawnTimer <= 0) { spawnCar(lane); lane.spawnTimer = lane.spawnInterval + (Math.random() - 0.5) * lane.spawnJitter; }
guidelines.originated: 2026-04-27 frogger-132 — cars + logs spawned at random visible X instead of off-screen; player can't react

guidelines.title: Forced-perspective sports games: screen Y is DEPTH, not HEIGHT — no gravity along screen-Y
guidelines.rule: When a game shows a forced-perspective top-down lane (Skee-Ball, bowling, shuffleboard, curling, basketball-at-hoop, mini-golf-putt, billiards-cue-end, beer-pong, ring-toss, darts-from-line), the screen Y-axis represents WORLD DEPTH (distance into the lane), NOT vertical height. The ball/disc rolls horizontally on a flat plane and gravity acts perpendicular to the screen — which means **gravity does NOT pull the ball back toward the player along screen-Y**. The ONLY deceleration is rolling friction. NEVER write `vy += GRAVITY` against screen-Y in this class of game.
guidelines.why: Real incident 2026-04-28 Skee-Ball canary: LLM modeled the lane as a 2D platformer, set `GRAVITY=0.14` on screen-vy, and wrote `ball.vy += GRAVITY`. Result: when the player flicks UP (vy negative, ball moves toward target rings at top of screen), gravity decelerates the ball, brings it to rest, then accelerates it BACK toward the player. The ball never reaches the rings no matter how hard the player flicks — it 'rises and falls' as if thrown straight up under gravity. This is a category error: in a forced-perspective top-down view, screen-Y is a projection of world-depth (Z in 3D), not world-Y (height). World gravity points along world-Y which is OUT OF the screen, so it has zero component along screen-Y. The bug is easy to write because 'ball + arc + gravity' is the LLM's strongest physics pattern and forced-perspective is rarer training data. The fix is structural: model the ball as moving on a 2D top-down plane where vy is depth-velocity, vx is lateral-velocity, and the only deceleration is friction (vy *= friction; vx *= friction).
examples: // BAD: 2D-platformer physics applied to a forced-perspective top-down lane
// var GRAVITY = 0.14;
// ball.vy += GRAVITY;  // ← pulls ball back toward player; ball NEVER reaches target
// ball.x += ball.vx; ball.y += ball.vy;
// CORRECT: top-down rolling physics — gravity is perpendicular to screen, only friction decelerates
var ROLL_FRICTION = 0.985;  // tune so a strong flick reaches the far end in ~1.2-1.8s
function stepBall(ball) {
ball.vx *= ROLL_FRICTION;
ball.vy *= ROLL_FRICTION;  // depth-velocity decays from rolling friction ONLY
ball.x += ball.vx;
ball.y += ball.vy;       // NOTE: no `ball.vy += GRAVITY` — there is no gravity along screen-Y
if (Math.hypot(ball.vx, ball.vy) < 0.3) settleAndScore(ball);
// If you want the ball to APPEAR to rise off the lane into the target ring (like real Skee-Ball's
// lip), use a SEPARATE perceived-Z that fakes the arc visually but doesn't affect lane physics:
// ballScreenY = ballLaneY - perceivedHeight(ballLaneY);  // perceivedHeight() arcs only near the lip
// Lane physics still operates on (vx, vy) with friction-only decel.
guidelines.originated: 2026-04-28 skee-ball-152 — LLM applied 2D-platformer gravity to screen-vy; ball flicked UP rises, slows, falls back to start. User: 'instead of going INTO the screen and landing in the holes, ball rises up vertically and then falls back to the ground back to the bottom of the screen'.

guidelines.title: Forced-perspective targets: hit-test in WORLD coordinates, not screen pixels
guidelines.rule: When the target (Skee-Ball rings, dart board, basketball hoop, bowling pins, curling button) is drawn at the perspective-far end of the lane, the visible pixel size is SMALLER than the world hit-radius because of foreshortening. Hit-test against the WORLD radius at the ball's depth, not against the rendered ring's screen radius. Rule of thumb: at lane-far (small screen-y), the world target radius corresponds to a LARGER hit-tolerance in pixels than the rendered ring suggests; at lane-near it's smaller. Without this, players who 'visually' land in the 100-ring score 0 because their pixel-distance to the ring center is larger than the rendered-ring radius.
guidelines.why: Companion rule to ag-019. After fixing the gravity model, the next pitfall is scoring: the rendered rings are drawn small (perspective-far), but the actual tolerance for 'this ball hit the 100' should be the ball's lane footprint vs. the world ring. Naive `dist = hypot(ball.x - ringCx, ball.y - ringCy); if (dist < ring.r) score = ring.pts;` works when ring.r is in world units but fails when ring.r is the small RENDERED radius. Either render rings at world-size (bigger pixels at the lane-far end, which looks weird in forced-perspective) OR keep two radii: `ring.renderR` (for drawing) and `ring.worldR` (for hit-test, larger).
examples: // CORRECT: separate render-radius from hit-radius
var RINGS = [
{ worldR: 22, renderR: 18, pts: 100 },  // hit-test forgiving; render small for perspective
{ worldR: 38, renderR: 32, pts: 50 },
{ worldR: 56, renderR: 48, pts: 30 },
{ worldR: 78, renderR: 68, pts: 20 },
{ worldR: 100, renderR: 88, pts: 10 }
function score(ball) {
var d = Math.hypot(ball.x - RING_CX, ball.y - RING_CY);
for (var r of RINGS) if (d <= r.worldR) return r.pts;  // hit-test on worldR
return 0;
// Rendering uses renderR for the visible perspective look:
// ctx.arc(RING_CX, RING_CY, ring.renderR, 0, Math.PI * 2);
guidelines.originated: 2026-04-28 skee-ball-152 followup — even after fixing gravity, naive radius hit-test against perspective-shrunk render-radius produces 'visually in the ring but scored 0' frustration.
